title: Are fraud disputes even arbitrable - the current legal position
p1:
Can allegations of fraud automatically push a dispute out of arbitration?
Most people assume courts alone can handle fraud cases—but that’s not the full picture.
p2:
Misconception: fraud disputes are never arbitrable.
The reality is more nuanced. Courts have repeatedly clarified that not every allegation of fraud belongs in a courtroom.

In many cases, especially where fraud is “simple” or between commercial parties, arbitration is allowed. The surprising part—this was strongly reinforced in recent Supreme Court rulings, which narrowed the exceptions significantly.
p3:
Key shift: Supreme Court decisions like Avitel Post Studioz v HSBC and Vidya Drolia have made the test clearer—what matters is whether the fraud is so serious that it undermines the arbitration agreement itself or demands a full criminal-style trial.

Practical takeaway: don’t assume “fraud = court case”. Many disputes can still go to arbitration if they involve commercial evidence and not complex criminal investigation.
conclusion: Do you think fraud allegations should be decided by courts only, or can arbitration handle them fairly in most commercial disputes?
